title: Capturing Output: > and >>
description: Redirect standard output to files. Learn the crucial difference between overwriting and appending.

slides.title: The Single Greater-Than: `>` ✍️
slides.content:
# Write to a File (Overwrite)

The `>` operator redirects **stdout** to a file.

**Syntax:**
```bash
command > filename
```

### Critical Behavior: It DESTROYS First!
If `filename` already exists, `>` will **immediately truncate (empty) the file** before the command even runs.

```bash
echo "Important Data" > backup.txt   # Creates/overwrites backup.txt
echo "New Data" > backup.txt         # backup.txt now contains ONLY "New Data"
```

### Analogy: The Whiteboard 🧽
You want to write a new message. You grab the eraser, wipe the board completely clean, then write your message. The old message is **gone forever**.

slides.title: Try Overwrite
slides.content: Observe how > replaces the file content.
slides.codeSnippet:
echo "First line" > test.txt
cat test.txt
echo "Second line" > test.txt
cat test.txt

quizData.question: What happens if you run `echo "Hello" > file.txt` and `file.txt` already has 1000 lines of text?

options.text: The file is erased and contains only "Hello".
An error occurs because the file exists.

quizData.explanation: `>` truncates the file to zero bytes before writing the new output.

slides.title: The Double Greater-Than: `>>` 📎
slides.content:
# Append to a File

If you want to add data to the **end** of an existing file without deleting it, use `>>`.

```bash
echo "Log entry 1" >> app.log
echo "Log entry 2" >> app.log
```

### Behavior:
- If the file **doesn't exist**, `>>` creates it (just like `>`).
- If the file **exists**, it appends the new data to the end.

### Hacker Use Case: Logging Script Output
```bash
./scan_target.sh >> scan_history.log
```
This keeps a permanent, growing record of all your scans.

slides.title: Try Append
slides.content: Build a file line by line.
slides.codeSnippet:
echo "Line 1" > log.txt
echo "Line 2" >> log.txt
echo "Line 3" >> log.txt
cat log.txt

slides.title: ⚠️ The Silent Destroyer: `set -o noclobber`
slides.content:
## Prevent Accidental Overwrite

If you are paranoid about overwriting important files with `>`, you can enable **noclobber**.

```bash
set -o noclobber
```

Now, `>` will **refuse** to overwrite an existing file. To force an overwrite, use `>|`.

```bash
echo "Data" > important.txt   # Error: cannot overwrite existing file
echo "Data" >| important.txt  # Force overwrite
```

This is a great safety net for critical systems.

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Creating Empty Files with Redirection

You can use `>` to quickly create an empty file (or empty an existing one).

```bash
> newfile.txt
```

This redirects 'nothing' to the file, effectively touching it or truncating it. It's a common shorthand in scripts.
